answer:
The two freedoms point in opposite directions — one outward toward legal-social conditions, the other inward toward the self.

**Comte's Liberté** (Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété*, Vol I, Ch. 3):

Strongest formulation (p-86c544): "The principle of liberty is, on the contrary, that a man can never be legitimately possessed by another, and that the products of his labors belong only to him, so long as he has not freely alienated them. In the state of liberty, the man who does not himself have enough power to shelter himself from violence or extortion, is protected by the combined forces of society."

For Comte, liberté is entirely external: the systematic removal of every element of servitude — domestic and political — so that no person is treated as property and no laborer is deprived of the fruit of their work. He drives this home with a crucial distinction (p-025e2a): most peoples have *liberties* (partial restorations of specific faculties), but almost none possess *liberty*, because liberty only exists "when all the elements whose ensemble constitutes servitude have completely disappeared."

Unmet condition Comte identifies: the continued existence, in law and custom, of any mechanism by which one person may seize another's person or labor — backed by public authority. The legal system must positively protect every individual from violence or extortion; until it does so fully and for all, liberty is incomplete.

**Iqbal's Aazadi** (Bang-e-Dara, Parts 1–3):

Strongest formulation (Part 2, Ch. 27 *Visal*, p-456772): "By entering chains, I found my liberty, / My home was built upon the wreck of me." Freedom for Iqbal is paradoxical — it arrives through surrender to the Beloved (God), not through removal of external constraint. The Urdu original of Part 2 Ch. 21 (*Saqaliya*, p-ab88c0) states the civic dimension: "آدمي آزاد زنجير توہم سے ہوا" — humankind was freed from the chains of superstition — declaring the Islamic civilizational mission to have been exactly this liberation. Part 3 Ch. 25 (*Wataniyat*, p-a723b2) adds the anti-territorial edge: "To be confined to one place is calamity, / Live free within the ocean, like a fish at sea" — true freedom cannot be a geographic or national affair; it belongs to the Muslim as a member of an unbounded ummah.

Unmet condition Iqbal identifies: the spiritual collapse of the Muslim self (*khudi*). From Part 3 Ch. 22 (*Taleem aur iske Nataaij*, p-c70195): "We once believed education would bring solace and liberty; who knew that godlessness would come along with it?" Western education substitutes a false liberation (secularism/materialism) for the real one. From Part 3 Ch. 8 (p-37e39f): "The Muslims are now prisoners in sectarianism's chains." The unmet condition is the interior one — the revival of Muslim identity through tawhid and love; without that inner freedom, political independence is worthless.

**The contrast**: Comte's liberté is a social-legal condition — the state achieves it once institutions destroy every mechanism of possession. Iqbal's aazadi is spiritual-interior — the self achieves it by the annihilation of ego and submission to God, which paradoxically is the only act that can also break colonial and superstitious chains. Comte would consider Iqbal's freedom pre-political and incomplete; Iqbal would consider Comte's freedom merely external and therefore insufficient.
